figures.caption: Figure 3 Toolset optimization and tool generation during a curriculum learning on quantum chemistry tasks. Left: A flat toolset at an early stage. Right: After optimization, existing tools (coloured lines) are reorganized into hierarchical subcategories based on functional similarity, and newly generated tools (green, labelled new) are added at the root awaiting future reorganization.

Table 3 Average combined score (mean of accuracy + methodology/2 per question, averaged over 3 runs; ± denotes the sample standard deviation across the 3 runs; bold = best setup (ZS/TR/EO) for each model within each benchmark). Tool reuse (TR) yields the highest average on both benchmarks, with the largest gains for weaker models (e.g., Kimi: +16.5pp on quantum chemistry). Zero-shot (ZS) tool generation consistently outperforms the evaluator-only (EO) baseline, confirming that tool generation contributes beyond the evaluation loop alone.
Table 2 Quantum dynamics benchmark: 11 tasks across 3 categories, derived from the benchmark set of El Agente Cuántico (Gustin et al., 2025). Full task prompts are provided in Appendix B.
Table 11 Zero-shot vs. Tool-reuse vs. Evaluator Only: Quantum Chemistry
Table 6 Model API pricing (per 1M tokens, USD).
Table 9 Accuracy criteria for the quantum dynamics tasks. Each criterion Ci is scored 1 if the stated condition holds, 0 otherwise. The task score is C̄ = 1 N ∑N i=1 Ci.
Table 17 Per-Task Aggregated Metrics: Quantum Dynamics

Figure 1 Overview of El Agente Forjador. (A) We evaluate our system on quantum chemistry and quantum dynamics tasks, which span molecular structure optimization, thermochemistry & reactivity, many-body quantum dynamics, and quantum state preparation & control. (B) The Forjador workflow comprises four stages: tool analysis (decompose tasks and search existing toolset), tool generation (synthesize missing tools), task execution (compose tools and run pipelines), and solution evaluation (iteratively assess results). Tasks produce reports and reusable toolsets. (C) Software backend: coding agents and scientific software used in this work.

Figure 6 Frontier orbital visualizations generated by the newly forged visualize_molecular_orbitals.py tool: HOMO (πCC, left) and LUMO (π∗ CC, right) of ethylene in the xz-plane. The π → π∗ character of the dominant S1 transition is clearly visible from the nodal structure.

Table 7 Accuracy criteria for the quantum chemistry tasks. Per-property/per-molecule criteria are evaluated independently; full score (1.0) and partial score (0.5) conditions are shown; score 0 applies otherwise. Levels: L0 = level 0, L1 = level 1, L2 = level 2.
Table 16 Per-Task Aggregated Metrics: Quantum Chemistry

Table 15 Average Iterations per Question: Zero-shot vs. Tool-reuse vs. Evaluator Only
Table 14 Average Cost per Question: Zero-shot vs. Tool-reuse vs. Evaluator Only

Figure 2 Detailed system architecture of El Agente Forjador. The agent workspace (left) maintains a structured directory containing the task specification (question.md), the tool_smith/ and tools/ directories holding generated Python scripts and JSON metadata, and a report.md output file. Tool generation agents (centre top) comprise three roles: a tool analyzer that assesses task requirements and existing tool coverage, a tool generator that synthesizes new tools backed by package source code, and a tool reviewer that reviews and refines tools for correctness and alignment; a toolset optimizer refines tools. Task-solving agents (centre bottom) consist of a task executor that composes tools into executable pipelines and dispatches jobs via an MCP server, and a solution evaluator that validates outputs. Coding agent abilities (right) available to all agents include file read/modify, script execution, HPC job submission, and image viewing.

Figure 9 Quantum dynamics benchmark score heatmap. Rows correspond to model–condition–run combinations; columns correspond to the 11 quantum dynamics tasks. Colour scale: red (0%) through yellow (50%) to green (100%).

Table 18 Tool file modifications during execution, reported per iteration. Edit Existing Tools: iterations in which a pre-existing tool file was modified. Created New Tools: iterations in which at least one new tool file was written.

Figure 4 Relative Cost / Time / Accuracy radar plots for El Agente Forjador with five agent setups on the quantum chemistry tasks (top) and quantum dynamics tasks (bottom) with different tool generation strategies. Each axis is independently min–max normalized across all models and conditions, then rescaled to [0.1, 0.9]. Accuracy (higher = better); Speed (lower time = better); Frugality (lower cost = better). Each axis represents a model; the shaded area indicates the fraction of tasks solved. Tool reuse consistently expands the shaded area toward the frugality and speed axes while maintaining or improving accuracy, with the most pronounced gains for weaker models such as Kimi K2.5 via OpenCode and GPT-5.2-Codex via Codex.
Table 12 Zero-shot vs. Tool-reuse vs. Evaluator Only: Quantum Dynamics
Table 8 Methodology criteria for the quantum chemistry tasks.

Figure 10 Tool generation versus usage in three zero-shot runs, broken down by model and question set. Blue bars indicate tools that were both generated and used; red segments indicate tools that were generated but never invoked.

Figure 8 Quantum chemistry benchmark score heatmap. Rows correspond to model–condition–run combinations; columns correspond to the 13 quantum chemistry tasks. Colour scale: red (0%) through yellow (50%) to green (100%).

Figure 7 Figures generated by agents for case study 2: Lindblad dynamics of a 87Rb hyperfine qubit using the experimental hyperfine splitting, with the PySCF contact density serving as a diagnostic comparison rather than a quantitative input to the dynamics. Left: Excited-state population Pe(t) decays exponentially with T1 = 10/ω from both initial states (|e⟩ and (|e⟩+ |g⟩)/ √ 2). Right: Coherence |ρ01| decays on the shorter T2 = 5/ω timescale for the superposition state; the eigenstate |e⟩ generates no coherence. Solid lines are numerical QuTiP results; dashed lines are analytical Bloch-equation solutions, showing agreement to machine precision.
